source_paper_title: Indefinite Linear-Quadratic Partially Observed Mean-Field Game

context:
Page 14, Remark 4.13(ii) (Section 4.3 Feedback representation).

Noticing that Riccati equation (35) is an asymmetric indefinite equation with complex structure, its solvability is still a problem.
title: Solvability of asymmetric indefinite Riccati equation for mean-field games setting
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-time linear-quadratic (LQ) mean-field game/control setting on a finite horizon \([0,T]\). Let \(n,m\in\mathbb{N}\). Coefficients \(A_t,\bar A_t,D_t,\bar D_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), \(B_t,\bar B_t,F_t,\bar F_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}\), and running/terminal weight matrices \(Q_t\in\mathbb{S}^n\), \(R_t\in\mathbb{S}^m\), \(S_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}\), \(L_T\in\mathbb{S}^n\) are deterministic functions/matrices (not necessarily positive semidefinite; i.e., the problem may be indefinite). Constants \(\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3,\beta_1,\beta_2\in\mathbb{R}\) are given.

In the paper, a key step for obtaining an explicit feedback (closed-loop) representation of the decentralized equilibrium control uses a matrix-valued Riccati differential equation for an unknown matrix function \(\Sigma:[0,T]\to\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\). This Riccati equation is generally not symmetric and is indefinite.

Define the following \(\Sigma\)-dependent matrices (as stated in the paper):
1) \(R^e_t := (1-\beta_1)R_t + F_t^\top \Sigma_t\,(F_t+\bar F_t)\).
2) \(\Sigma^e_t := \Sigma_t\,(B_t+\bar B_t) + D_t^\top \Sigma_t\,(F_t+\bar F_t) + (1-\beta_2)S_t\).
3) \(\bar\Sigma_t := \Sigma_t\,\bar B_t + (D_t+\bar D_t)^\top \Sigma_t\,F_t + (1-\alpha_2)S_t\).

The terminal condition is \(\Sigma_T = (1-\alpha_3)L_T\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine verifiable conditions (or a general solvability theory) guaranteeing that the following matrix Riccati differential equation admits a (unique) solution \(\Sigma\) on \([0,T]\):
\[
\dot\Sigma_t + \Sigma_t(A_t+\bar A_t) + A_t^\top \Sigma_t + D_t^\top \Sigma_t(D_t+\bar D_t)
- \Sigma^e_t\,(R^e_t)^{-1}\,\bar\Sigma_t^{\top} + (1-\alpha_1)Q_t = 0,\qquad \Sigma_T=(1-\alpha_3)L_T,
\]
where \(R^e_t,\Sigma^e_t,\bar\Sigma_t\) are defined in the background, and the equation is allowed to be asymmetric and indefinite (i.e., no a priori sign definiteness of \(Q_t,R_t,L_T\) is assumed).
latex:

Coefficients \(A_t,\bar A_t,D_t,\bar D_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), \(B_t,\bar B_t,F_t,\bar F_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}\), and running/terminal weight matrices \(Q_t\in\mathbb{S}^n\), \(R_t\in\mathbb{S}^m\), \(S_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}\), \(L_T\in\mathbb{S}^n\) are deterministic functions/matrices (not necessarily positive semidefinite; i.e., the problem may be indefinite). Constants \(\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3,\beta_1,\beta_2\in\mathbb{R}\) are given.

In the paper, a key step for obtaining an explicit feedback (closed-loop) representation of the decentralized equilibrium control uses a matrix-valued Riccati differential equation for an unknown matrix function \(\Sigma:[0,T]\to\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\). Define the following \(\Sigma\)-dependent matrices (as stated in the paper):

2) \(\Sigma^e_t := \Sigma_t\,(B_t+\bar B_t) + D_t^\top \Sigma_t\,(F_t+\bar F_t) + (1-\beta_2)S_t\).
3) \(\bar\Sigma_t := \Sigma_t\,\bar B_t + (D_t+\bar D_t)^\top \Sigma_t\,F_t + (1-\alpha_2)S_t\).

\[

open_problem_quote: Noticing that Riccati equation (35) is an asymmetric indefinite equation with complex structure, its solvability is still a problem.
subject_classification: stochastic control and mean-field games (indefinite LQ, Riccati equations)
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for general solvability conditions for a matrix Riccati ODE with coefficient-dependent invertibility and asymmetry; it is not naturally a finite combinatorial search problem. Automated counterexample search would require fixing a parameterized family of coefficients and a precise candidate theorem to falsify.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Evaluating candidate solutions or conditions would require symbolic or analytic verification across continuous-time matrix ODEs and parameter families, without an obvious automated fitness function. One could numerically test specific coefficient instances, but that would not reliably validate general solvability claims.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
